questions.questionText: The primary active transport mechanism in most mammalian cells is the:
questions.explanation: Na+/K+ ATPase maintains the resting membrane potential and cell volume.

questions.questionText: In a normal resting cell, the cell membrane is most permeable to:
questions.explanation: Resting membrane is 50-100 times more permeable to K+ due to leak channels.

questions.questionText: Secondary active transport typically relies on the electrochemical gradient of:
questions.explanation: The Na+ gradient created by Na+/K+ ATPase drives most secondary active transport.

questions.questionText: The Gibbs-Donnan effect helps explain the uneven distribution of:
questions.optionA: Water molecules across a semipermeable membrane
questions.optionB: Diffusible ions across a membrane containing non-diffusible proteins
questions.explanation: Non-diffusible negatively charged proteins limit the diffusion of other ions.

questions.questionText: Apoptosis is characterized by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Apoptosis is programmed cell death and typically does not elicit inflammation.

questions.questionText: Which of the following cellular junctions prevents paracellular transport?
questions.explanation: Tight junctions seal adjacent cells together.

questions.questionText: The Nernst equation is used to calculate:
questions.optionB: Resting membrane potential for all ions
questions.optionC: Equilibrium potential for a single ion
questions.explanation: Nernst equation determines the potential at which there is no net movement of a specific ion.

questions.questionText: Facilitated diffusion differs from simple diffusion because it:
questions.explanation: Carrier proteins in facilitated diffusion can become saturated.
Potassium is the most abundant intracellular cation.

questions.questionText: Which receptor type is characterized by 7 transmembrane domains?
questions.explanation: GPCRs classically have 7 transmembrane alpha helices.

questions.questionText: In General Physiology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Physiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Physiology references for General Physiology. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: General Physiology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical General Physiology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: General Physiology.
